items.myth: Multinomial and Bernoulli Naive Bayes are the same model with different names.
items.reality: They use different feature encodings and different likelihood formulas: Multinomial NB sums contributions weighted by word counts; Bernoulli NB models binary presence and absence for every vocabulary word.
items.example: Document "win win win" adds 3·log θ_y,win under Multinomial NB but only log θ_y,win once under Bernoulli NB.

items.myth: Naive Bayes cannot work because words are obviously dependent.
items.reality: The independence assumption is wrong but often good enough for bag-of-words classification; the model can still rank classes correctly when class-conditional word frequencies differ.
items.example: Spam emails correlate "free" and "click," yet a Multinomial NB spam filter can still exceed 95% accuracy on SMS data despite violated independence.

items.myth: Higher word counts always help Multinomial NB regardless of document length.
items.reality: Very long documents inflate counts for common tokens unless you normalize, use stop words, or compare log scores with length-aware baselines.
items.example: A 10-page report repeating "the" thousands of times can skew scores unless stop words are removed or counts are normalized.
